findings.title: unsafe block without SAFETY comment
findings.description: Unsafe blocks should document why they're safe. Add a `// SAFETY:` comment.
findings.why_it_matters: Unsafe code bypasses Rust's safety guarantees. SAFETY comments are essential for code review.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 4 functions in call cycle (complexity 32)
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Mutual recursion detected: 4 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/proto/h1/role.rs::encode_headers:111 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/proto/h1/role.rs::impl<Http1Transaction for Server>::encode:363 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/proto/h1/role.rs::impl<Server>::encode_headers_with_lower_case:523 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/proto/h1/role.rs::impl<Server>::encode_headers_with_original_case:564. Aggregate complexity: 32.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Panic risk: unwrap() without context
findings.description: Using `unwrap()` can cause panics. Consider `?`, `unwrap_or`, or proper error handling.
findings.why_it_matters: Panics crash the program without recovery. Using proper error handling makes code more robust and debuggable.

findings.title: Architectural Bottleneck: bench (architectural hub)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'bench' is called by 19 functions and has complexity 17. Changes here are high-risk.

Betweenness centrality: 0.2449 - this function sits on many execution paths between other functions.

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 17 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=6, p95=8, mean=2.6, n=1037
findings.suggested_fix: This is a critical hub. Ensure comprehensive test coverage, add defensive error handling, and consider circuit breaker pattern.
findings.why_it_matters: Bottlenecks are single points of failure that amplify bugs. High fan-in with high complexity means many callers depend on risky code.

findings.title: High panic density in `bench`: 9 panic-family calls
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function `bench` contains 9 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 292, line 314, line 322, line 328, line 333, line 339, line 348, line 355, line 380. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: High panic density in `main`: 4 panic-family calls
findings.description: Function `main` contains 4 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 37, line 39, line 54, line 56. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Structural bridge: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/common/buf.rs::impl<BufList<T>>::bufs_cnt:24` separates components of [1151, 14]
findings.description: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/common/buf.rs::impl<BufList<T>>::bufs_cnt:24` is a structural bridge. Removing it would split the graph into components of sizes [1151, 14]. All communication between these components currently flows through this single node.
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce coupling through this node by introducing alternative call paths, extracting shared interfaces, or splitting the bridge function into independently accessible units.
findings.why_it_matters: A structural bridge is a single node whose failure disconnects entire subsystems. This creates fragile architecture where a bug, API change, or refactor in one function can cascade to all dependent components.

findings.title: Mutex poisoning risk
findings.description: Using .lock().unwrap() will panic if the mutex is poisoned. Consider handling the PoisonError or using parking_lot::Mutex.
findings.suggested_fix:
Handle poisoning: `mutex.lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())`
Or use `parking_lot::Mutex` which has no poisoning.
findings.why_it_matters: Poisoned mutexes cause cascading panics across threads.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 3 functions in call cycle (complexity 22)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 3 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/proto/h2/ping.rs::impl<KeepAlive>::maybe_ping:456 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/proto/h2/ping.rs::impl<KeepAlive>::maybe_timeout:482 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/proto/h2/ping.rs::impl<Ponger>::poll:265. Aggregate complexity: 22.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Structural bridge: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/rt/io.rs::impl<ReadBufCursor<'_>>::as_mut:331` separates components of [1154, 11]
findings.description: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/hyper/src/rt/io.rs::impl<ReadBufCursor<'_>>::as_mut:331` is a structural bridge. Removing it would split the graph into components of sizes [1154, 11]. All communication between these components currently flows through this single node.
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce coupling through this node by introducing alternative call paths, extracting shared interfaces, or splitting the bridge function into independently accessible units.
findings.why_it_matters: A structural bridge is a single node whose failure disconnects entire subsystems. This creates fragile architecture where a bug, API change, or refactor in one function can cascade to all dependent components.

findings.title: High file-level panic density: 12 panic-family calls
findings.description: File `benches/end_to_end.rs` contains 12 total panic-family calls (`.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, `panic!()`) outside of test code. Consider refactoring to reduce panic surface.
findings.suggested_fix:
Consider:
- Introducing a module-level error type
- Replacing unwrap chains with `?` propagation
- Using `anyhow::Context` for better error messages
findings.why_it_matters: Files with many panic points are hard to maintain and make the overall application fragile. Centralizing error handling improves reliability.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 8 levels in decode
findings.description:
8 levels of nesting (threshold: 7).

**Analysis:**
In function: `decode`
High complexity: 21 (nesting compounds this)
Contains 1 match/switch level(s) (discounted from raw depth 9)
Existing helpers that could reduce nesting: read_mem

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 8 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=3, p95=4, mean=1.0, n=1037
findings.suggested_fix:
This function already has helpers like `read_mem`. Consider:
1. Extract more nested blocks into similar helpers
2. Use guard clauses (early returns) to reduce nesting
3. Replace nested ifs with switch/match
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Long method: parse (224 lines)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'parse' has 224 lines (effective threshold: 150).

**Graph Analysis:**
Orchestrator pattern: calls 7 functions (reduced severity)
Calls 3 different modules - possible split points
findings.suggested_fix:
This appears to be an orchestrator function (coordinates many calls).
If it must remain long, ensure it:
1. Has clear section comments
2. Handles errors at each step
3. Has a clear flow (consider a state machine for complex flows)
findings.why_it_matters: Long methods are hard to understand, test, and maintain. Each function should do one thing well.
